coredata.dc:title:
Exploring the thermal attributes of nano-composition (GQDs+Bi
2
Se
3
+Ag) suspended in therminol VP-1: An artificial intelligence based approach
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Ahmad, Sohail
Alsalmah, Hessa A.
coredata.dc:description:
Efficient thermal management is required in advanced engineering applications such as energy systems, electronics cooling, and industrial processes. The exceptional thermal properties of graphene quantum dots GQDs combined with the thermoelectric performance of bismuth selenide Bi
2
Se
3 and the high conductivity of silver Ag provide significant advancements in heat transfer efficiency and thermal control systems. We explore, in this study, the novel thermal attributes of a ternary nano-composition consisting of GQDs + Bi
2
Se
3
+Ag particles suspended in Therminol VP-1. The incorporation of thermal radiation and activation energy offers insights into the temperature-sensitive processes. The analysis covers the features of three types of nano-compositions such as GQDs/Therminol VP-1, GQDs-Bi
2
Se
3
/Therminol VP-1 and, GQDs-Bi
2
Se
3
-Ag/Therminol VP-1. An order reduction approach is applied to streamline the mathematical modelling and computational efforts while preserving the system's accuracy. The analysis incorporates a machine learning technique based on recurrent neural network (RNN) to evaluate the nonlinear impacts of the physical parameters. The outcomes evidently disclose the fact that the volume concentration
Φ 2
of bismuth selenide and
Φ 3
of silver tend to elevate the temperature in usual, hybridized and tri-hybridized cases of nano-compositions. The heat transfer rate increased up to 24.5 % when the volume concentration
Φ 2
of bismuth selenide and
Φ 3
of silver increased up to 0.7 and 0.3 respectively. The activation energy
A E
substantially promoted the concentration in either case of nano-composition e.g., ternary, binary and pure nano-composition case.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Recurrent neural network
Graphene quantum dots
Bismuth selenide
Ternary nano-composition
Order reduction technique

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Training RNNs for the concerned problem
Recurrent neural network analysis of the problem

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The structure of the problem.
The artificial neural network mechanism.
The flowchart for RNNs.
Regression diagram.
The training state diagram.
Best performance diagram.
Nusselt number determined by order reduction and RNN method.
Absolute error determined by order reduction and RNN method.
Temperature determined by order reduction and RNN method.
Variation in velocity with magnetic parameter.
Variation in temperature with magnetic parameter.
Variation in velocity with suction parameter.
Variation in temperature with suction parameter.
Variation in temperature with radiation parameter.
Variation in temperature with volume fraction of Bismuth selenide.
Variation in temperature with volume fraction of silver.
Variation in concentration with chemical reaction.
Variation in concentration with activation energy.

Values for the thermal properties of GODs, Bi
2
Se
3 , Ag and Therminol VP-1.

Error change in
F
( η )
subject to step size h.
Variation in surface drag with suction and magnetic parameter when
Variation in heat transfer rate with volume concentration of Bismuth selenide, silver and thermal radiation.
Variation in mass transfer rate with chemical reaction and activation energy.

Quantitative results for the volume concentration
Φ 2
and
Φ 3
.

head.ce:title:
Exploring the thermal attributes of nano-composition (GQDs+Bi
2
Se
3
+Ag) suspended in therminol VP-1: An artificial intelligence based approach

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Basic Sciences and Humanities, Muhammad Nawaz Sharif University of Engineering and Technology, Multan, 60000, Pakistan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Basic Sciences and Humanities
Muhammad Nawaz Sharif University of Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Basic Sciences and Humanities, Muhammad Nawaz Sharif University of Engineering and Technology, Multan 60000, Pakistan.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, College of Science, Imam Mohammad Ibn Saud Islamic University (IMSIU), Riyadh, 11623, Saudi Arabia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Imam Mohammad Ibn Saud Islamic University (IMSIU)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, College of Science, Imam Mohammad Ibn Saud Islamic University (IMSIU), Riyadh 11623, Saudi Arabia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Efficient thermal management is required in advanced engineering applications such as energy systems, electronics cooling, and industrial processes. The exceptional thermal properties of graphene quantum dots GQDs combined with the thermoelectric performance of bismuth selenide Bi
2
Se
3 and the high conductivity of silver Ag provide significant advancements in heat transfer efficiency and thermal control systems. We explore, in this study, the novel thermal attributes of a ternary nano-composition consisting of GQDs + Bi
2
Se
3
+Ag particles suspended in Therminol VP-1. The incorporation of thermal radiation and activation energy offers insights into the temperature-sensitive processes. The analysis covers the features of three types of nano-compositions such as GQDs/Therminol VP-1, GQDs-Bi
2
Se
3
/Therminol VP-1 and, GQDs-Bi
2
Se
3
-Ag/Therminol VP-1. An order reduction approach is applied to streamline the mathematical modelling and computational efforts while preserving the system's accuracy. The analysis incorporates a machine learning technique based on recurrent neural network (RNN) to evaluate the nonlinear impacts of the physical parameters. The outcomes evidently disclose the fact that the volume concentration
Φ 2
of bismuth selenide and
Φ 3
of silver tend to elevate the temperature in usual, hybridized and tri-hybridized cases of nano-compositions. The heat transfer rate increased up to 24.5 % when the volume concentration
Φ 2
of bismuth selenide and
Φ 3
of silver increased up to 0.7 and 0.3 respectively. The activation energy
A E
substantially promoted the concentration in either case of nano-composition e.g., ternary, binary and pure nano-composition case.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
In the recent years, the enhancement of heat transfer fluids has been a focal point in thermal management research. The utilization of nanomaterials into base fluids like Therminol VP-1 has shown promise in improving thermal properties. Therminol VP-1 is a synthetic heat transfer fluid composed of a eutectic mixture of diphenyl oxide and biphenyl. It is renowned for its exceptional thermal stability and low viscosity. The inclusion of nanoparticles like graphene quantum dots (GQDs), bismuth selenide (Bi
2
Se
3 ), and silver (Ag) can significantly increase the thermal conductivity of Therminol VP-1. This combination leads towards more efficient heat transfer in industrial applications. The (GQDs + Bi2Se3 + Ag) nano-composite-enhanced Therminol VP-1 has several high-performance thermal management applications such as concentrated solar power (CSP) systems, electronic cooling, nuclear and thermal power plants, advanced heat exchangers, thermoelectric devices, and automotive and aerospace thermal systems.

Machine learning enables precise estimations of heat transfer performance and optimization of nanofluid-based thermal systems by employing data-driven approaches. Bonab and Asrami [1] described the urgent requirement of effective heat transport mechanisms by concentrating primarily on critical heat flux (CHF) in nanofluids. Four predictive models were established that were solved with the help of machine learning algorithms. This research examined that how the heat flux is affected by the nanofluid parameters. The results of this study could be used in more efficient thermal management systems. Singh et al. [2] illustrated the thermal attributes of nanofluids employing artificial intelligence-based model. It was claimed that the AI-model would be cost and time saving for determining the thermo-physical characteristics of several nanofluids. These properties could be forecasted easily with this approach.

The flow of Casson hybrid nanofluid comprising of gold and iron oxide particles was investigated by Ramasekhar et al. [3] using feed-forward back-propagation artificial neural network. The Fehlberg method in MATLAB was incorporated for the solution of governing model system of equations. The viscosity of nanofluids was predicted by AbuShanab et al. [4] using machine learning models. The nanoparticles of Polyalpha-Olefin-hexagonal boron nitride (PAO-hBN) was taken for this purpose. The major goal of this research was to use three different machine learning models to asses and predict the nanofluid viscosity. Shateri et al. [5] proposed a committee machine intelligent system (CMIS), which is basically a machine learning model, to evaluate the nanofluid's physical properties. A change in volume fraction led to a significant enhancement in the viscosity.

Trihybrid nanofluids represent a significant advancement in heat transfer technology. Rabby et al. [6] illustrated the thermo-hydraulic attributes of tri-hybrid nanofluids using three distinct nanoparticles. This study covered the various aspects like microscopic evaluation, preparation processes, and stability of ternary nanofluids. Panwar et al. [7] conducted a review on innovations in flexible sensory devices designed to assist the visually impaired. Shinwari et al. [8] incorporated copper, silver and ferric oxide nanoparticles to prepare the ternary mixture Fe
3
O
4
-Cu-Ag amalgamated in ethylene glycol (C
2
H
6
O
2 ). Equal particles concentration (almost 2 %) was taken for all nanoparticles. The entropy rates and Bejan number had been physically interpreted in this research. Faisal and Rauf [9] investigated the mass and heat transport Maxwell ternary nanofluid flow utilizing Levenberg-Marquardt ANN technique and Bvp5c numerical algorithm. According to the results, the Nusselt number was improved by raising the temperature-dependent thermal conductivity parameter from 0.4 to 2.5 and the permeability parameter from 1 to 1.2. The analysis of Al
2
O
3
+Cu + TiO
2
/EO with fuzzy volume fraction was performed by Zulqarnain et al. [10]. The scenario was explored for Riga wedge incorporating slip boundary conditions. The engine oil worked as host liquid in the ternary mixture. The flow rate was noticed high for Maxwell fluid parameter, nonlinear Grashof numbers, and Darcy-Forchheimer parameter. The electromagnetohydrodynamic flow across a microchannel was studied by Elsaid et al. [11] incorporating three distinct nanoparticles. In contrast to the irreversibility caused by thermal transfer, the irreversibility caused by friction and the electric and magnetic fields obviously increased in the center and decreased toward the walls. Kharangarh et al. [12,13] investigated the luminescent and nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots for energy-related applications.

Many researchers explored the activation energy in the dynamics of several nanomaterials. A Carreau fluid was taken into consideration by Zeb et al. [14] under the melting heat transfer and activation energy. The partial differential equations (PDEs) described the physical phenomenon of the problem. The accuracy of the numerical outcomes was judged through a comparison. Rekha et al. [15] presented a numerical investigation that expressed the flow of alloys (AA7072 and AA7075) in the coexistence of activation energy. Three distinct geometries like plate, cone, and wedge were incorporated. It was a comparative analysis in which thermal features of fluid were observed. It was deduced that the temperature would be minimum when the flow past the cone. Reddy et al. [16] assessed the second-grade nanoliquid with heat generation/absorption and activation energy. An ND-Solver was the source of numerical iterations. The heat source parameter strongly diminished the Nusselt number. A Marangoni driven flow of second-grade non-Newtonian liquid comprising of activation energy was reported by Gowda et al. [17]. The results based on numerical simulation were interpreted physically. The velocity gradient increased due to the Marangoni convection. Reddy et al. [18] utilized bvp4c MATLAB built-in-function to explore the flow and thermal characteristics of MHD Williamson nanofluid flow. The impact of radiation parameter as well as activation energy was also deliberated. The Grashof number and the Eckert number were two factors that caused an increase in the temperature of fluid.

The aim of this work is to provide innovative research in the field of heat transfer fluids enhanced with nanomaterials. The proposed nanomaterials have unique characteristics. Graphene quantum dots GQDs are known for their superior thermal conductivity and stability. In the same way, bismuth selenide (Bi
2
Se
3 ) and silver are recognized for their thermoelectric properties. However, the amalgamation GQDs + Bi
2
Se
3
+Ag suspended in Therminol VP-1 has not been investigated earlier. The study employs recurrent neural networks (RNNs), a class of artificial neural networks adept at processing sequential data, to model and predict the thermal behaviour of the proposed nano-compositions GQDs + Bi
2
Se
3
+Ag. In the present work, we assessed that how the addition of GQDs, Bi
2
Se
3 , and Ag nanoparticles influences the overall thermal efficiency of Therminol VP-1. This research aims to advance the knowledge of how specific nanomaterial additives can improve the performance of heat transfer fluids and to develop computational algorithms for optimizing their behaviour in practical applications.

The nanoparticles (GQDs, Bi
2
Se
3 , and Ag) are presumed to be uniformly dispersed in the base fluid. That is why there is no particle agglomeration or sedimentation. This homogeneous distribution causes the stable and consistent thermal and electrical properties. Due to the conductive nature of bismuth selenide and silver, the magnetic field may cause electric currents to flow through the nanofluid. This phenomenon generates a Lorentz force that affects the flow. The strength of the magnetic field and the nanofluid's electric conductivity are used to model this Lorentz force. A force
B →
0
is applied in the same direction where the y-axis is taken (see Fig. 1 ). The Brownian motion and thermophoresis are assumed to be negligible due to the larger nanoparticles Bi
2
Se
3 and Ag. However, the Brownian motion may be taken depending on particle size and flow conditions. For example, it can be assumed for the smaller nanoparticles like GQDs.

The model equations describe the magnetohydrodynamic ternary nanofluid flow comprising of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), bismuth selenide (Bi
2
Se
3 ), and silver (Ag) nanoparticles dispersed in Therminol VP-1 as the base fluid [19–22].

According to the flow premises, the momentum equation can be composed as:
(1)

u ,

The modified or derived version of the governing system, which is connected to the magnetic field term, is represented by equation (1). The applied magnetic field effect is denoted by the final term of the same equation. The inertia forces are represented by
u
∂ u
∂ x
and
v
∂ u
∂ y
on the left side of Equation (1). These forces' primary function is to prevent particles from entering the layer. Conversely, the viscous force
υ

∂
y 2
(on the right side) is responsible for the layers' smooth movement over one another.

,

An essential tool for comprehending the connection between temperature, activation energy, and reaction rate in chemical kinetics is the Arrhenius Equation. It demonstrates that reactions with lower activation energies continue more easily and that the rate of reaction grows exponentially with temperature. The Arrhenius equation explains how reaction rates depend on temperature and activation energy. It is frequently associated with the concentration equation using activation energy. The Arrhenius Equation is given by:
(4)
k = A
e
−
−
E ∗
R T
Where.
A is the frequency factor (pre-exponential factor),
k is the rate constant of the reaction,
R is the gas constant,

E ∗
is the activation energy,
T is the temperature.

The exponential term
e
−
−
E ∗
R T
becomes larger (closer to 1) as the temperature rises. It means that more molecules have sufficient energy to surpass the activation energy barrier. So, the concentration equation under these premises can be written as:
(5)
u
∂ C
∂ x
+ v
∂ C
∂ y
=
D B
∂ 2
C
∂
y 2
−
K r 2
(
C −
C ∞
)
(
T
T ∞
)
m 0
exp
(
−
E ∗
k ∗
T
)
Where
K r
is the rate constant of reaction,
m o
is the exponent fixed rate,
C
is the fluid's concentration and,
D B
represents the mass diffusivity.

The fixed conditions on the boundary are given as [26]:
(6)

}
Whereas the factors like
U w
,
v 0
,
C w
, and
T w
express the velocity, suction, concentration and temperature at
y = 0
(on the surface). Likewise, the terms
T ∞
and
C ∞
are quantified at
y → ∞
(away from surface).

The nano-composition of three distinct types of nanoparticles (GQDs + Bi
2
Se
3
+Ag) tends to generate the ternary nanofluids whereas GQDs + Bi
2
Se
3 causes the hybrid nanofluid creation. The analysis involves three types of nanofluids.
(i)
Graphene quantum dots together with host liquid Therminol VP-1 makes the pure nanofluid,
(ii)
The hybrid case involves the mixture of graphene quantum dots and bismuth selenide in the Therminol VP-1,
(iii)
The amalgamation of GQDs + Bi
2
Se
3
+Ag in Therminol VP-1 gives rise to the ternary hybrid nanofluid.

The fundamental properties of the above-described nanoparticles are appeared in Table 1 . Whereas the mathematical relations for the tri-hybrid nanofluid's thermophysical characteristics are pledged from the available researches [27–29] and, are specified as follows:

.

An order reduction and finite differences are consolidated to numerically tackle the nonlinear problem. Order reduction is a technique which is often used to lower the order of a differential equation. This method can reduce the order of differential equations of order n to a system of required order equations which can be solved easily using numerical methods.

The third-order ordinary differential equation (9) of the problem is:
(19)
P ‴
=
C 0
+
C 1
(
P ′
2
− P
P ″
)
+
C 2
M
P ′
,

Put
P ′
=
d P
d η
=
L ′
in above to reduce its order by one:
(20)
L ″
=
C 0
+
C 1
(
f ′
2
− f
L ′
)
+
C 2
M L ,

Now, we will have to solve the following system of second-order differential equations instead of solving third-order equations:
(21)
L ″
=
C 0
+
C 1
(
L 2
− f
L ′
)
+
C 2
M L ,
(22)
1 Pr
(
K
T n f
K f
+
4 3
T R
)
θ ″
+
C 4
P
θ ′
= 0 ,
(23)
1
S c
ϕ ″
+ P
ϕ ′
−
γ R
(
1 + δ θ
)
m 1
exp
(
−
A E
1 + δ θ
)
ϕ = 0

Subject to BCs:
(24)
a t
ξ = 0 : P = S n ,
P ′
= 1 , Θ = 1 , ϕ = 1 ,
a t
ξ → ∞ :
P ′
→ 0 , Θ → 0 , ϕ → 0 .
}

The finite difference method (FDM) solves the differential equations by using difference formulas to approximate derivatives. To do this, the continuous domain is discretized into a finite set of points. Afterward, the derivatives are subsequently substituted with finite differences. The finite difference approximations for an ODE of the form
d y
d x
= f
(
x , y
)
at a point
x i
can be specified as:
(25)
Forward
difference :
y ′
(
x i
)
≈
y
(
x
i + 1
)
− y
(
x i
)
h
(26)
Backward
difference :
y ′
(
x i
)
≈
y
(
x i
)
− y
(
x
i − 1
)
h
(27)
Central
difference :
y ′
(
x i
)
≈
y
(
x
i + 1
)
− y
(
x
i − 1
)
2 h

Using the central differences in (21)–(23) for
L , θ
and
ϕ
will yield an algebraic system that will be solved iteratively in MATLAB. A comparison that confirms the accuracy of the numerical process and validates the findings is shown in Table 2 . This Table displays the comparative results of the numerical uncertainty analysis. This table can be used to analyze the numerical values of
F
( η )
that correlate to the step size h.

RNNs process sequences of data by maintaining a "memory" of previous computations, making them useful for modeling time-independent phenomena like steady nanofluid flow. They use a feedback mechanism that allows the current output to depend on both the current input and the output from the previous step. The artificial neural network's mechanism is exhibited in Fig. 2 a.

In a nanofluid flow problem, variables such as temperature, velocity, and concentration at time
t
are influenced by their values at earlier times. RNNs can capture these dependencies effectively. An RNN computes the hidden state
h t
and output
y t
at each time step
t
as follows:
(28)
h t
= f
(
W h
−
h
t − 1
+
W x
x t
+
b h
)
(29)
y t
= g
(
W y
h t
+
b y
)
where.

h t
: Hidden state at time
t
, representing memory of the system,
x t
: Input vector at time
t
, e.g., flow parameters (temperature, velocity),
W h
,
W x
: Weight matrices for hidden and input states, respectively,
b h
: Bias term,
f
: Nonlinear activation function (e.g.,
tanh
),
y t
: Output vector at time
t
, e.g., predicted flow variables,
W y
: Weight matrix for output,
b y
: Bias term,
g
: output activation function.

Nanofluid flow dynamics often involve complex interactions between variables including temperature, velocity, and concentration which vary over time and are influenced by boundary conditions and external forces. The RNN's ability to retain memory and learn sequential dependencies makes it a powerful tool for such problems.

ce:list-item.ce:para:
Governing equations like the Navier-Stokes equations describe the flow dynamics and temperature e.g.,
∂ u
∂ x
,
∂ u
∂ y
,
∂ T
∂ x
,
∂ T
∂ y
.

RNNs naturally handle the above dependencies by updating the hidden state
h t
recursively, reflecting the effect of past flow states.
Nanofluid flows often involve nonlinear phenomena due to the complex interaction of nanoparticles with the base fluid.
The nonlinear activation functions in RNNs (e.g., tanh\tanhtanh, ReLU) enable them to approximate such nonlinear behaviors effectively.
Initial and boundary conditions influence future states in fluid dynamics.
By training on sequences that incorporate these conditions, RNNs can model their effects over time.

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 b depicts the structural flowchart of RNNs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Training RNNs for the concerned problem
ce:section.ce:para:
RNNs are trained using supervised learning, where the model is provided with input-output pairs (e.g., temperature profiles over time). The loss function, typically mean squared error (MSE) for regression tasks, is minimized using backpropagation through time (BPTT):
L =
1 T
∑
t = 1
T
(
y t
t r u e
−
y t
p r e d
)
2
Here,
y t
t r u e
: True values (e.g., observed flow parameters) and
y t
p r e d
: Predicted values from the RNN. Predicting the temperature in the concerned problem involves the following data:

Inputs: the inputs are parameters like
Φ 1
,
Φ 3
,
Φ 2
,
T R
, and
A E
.

Outputs: Temperature
θ
( η )
.
Training: Simulation data of flow parameters.
Prediction: The RNN predicts future states of temperature given initial conditions and driving forces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recurrent neural network analysis of the problem
ce:section.ce:para: Recurrent neural networks (RNNs) are designed to process sequential data by maintaining a hidden state that captures temporal dependencies. The RNNs can perform regression tasks by learning continuous outputs. The RNN characteristics (e.g., regression, training state and best performance) can be observed from Fig. 3 a–c . By 217 epochs, the loss (e.g., mean squared error) have been stabilized. It reflects the RNN's ability to learn from the tri-hybrid nanofluid flow data. The gradients are likely well-behaved at this stage, avoiding vanishing or exploding issues due to architectural enhancements like Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) or Gated Recurrent Units (GRUs). RNNs use training via backpropagation through time for gradient calculation which can face challenges like vanishing/exploding gradients. They retain information from previous time steps via hidden states, making them ideal for capturing patterns in dynamic systems like tri-hybrid nanofluid flows.

The training error (e.g., MSE) is at its minimum which designates that the model effectively fits the data. Fig. 4 a and b determine the Nusselt number and absolute error (in temperature) calculated by the proposed order reduction numerical scheme and recurrent neural network. A low validation error reflects good generalization. The RNN effectively predicts flow characteristics under varying conditions which achieves high correlation with simulation data. The model captures both short-term fluctuations and long-term dependencies in the trihybrid nanofluid flow system. The temperature determined by order reduction and RNN method is portrayed in Fig. 4 c.

RNNs are particularly suitable for modelling the trihybrid nanofluids due to the system's temporal and nonlinear nature. They can predict heat transfer rates, velocity profiles, and shear stress which are influenced by nanoparticle concentration, temperature gradients, and flow conditions.

The interpretations of the numerical results are presented in this section. The problem parameters are:
Φ 1
_volume concentration of graphene quantum dots,
S c =
υ f
D B
_ Schmidt number,
T R
=
4
σ 0
T ∞ 3
k 0
k f
_ thermal radiation parameter,
A E
=
E ∗
k ∗
T ∞
_activation energy,
Pr =
μ f
(
c p
)
f
k f
_ Prandtl number,
Φ 2
_ nanoparticles volume concentration of bismuth selenide,
γ R
=
K r
x
U w
_ chemical reaction parameter,
M 2
=
σ f
B 0 2
c
ρ f
_ magnetic interaction parameter,
Φ 3
_ nanoparticles volume concentration of silver, and
S n
=
v 0
c
υ f
_suction parameter. The value of
M = 1.5
and
S n
= 0.8
are used in the iterative process. Whereas the remaining parameters attain the values stated in Table 4. The combination of the proposed nanoparticles’ volume concentrations (in the present analysis) is portrayed as:

Pure nano fluid:
Φ 2
=
Φ 3
= 0
and
Φ 1
= 0.03
,

Hybrid nano fluid:
Φ 1
= 0.03 ,
Φ 2
= 0.05
and
Φ 3
= 0
,

Tri-hybrid nano fluid:
Φ 1
= 0.03 ,
Φ 2
= 0.05 ,
Φ 3
= 0.1
.

It is to be noticed from Table 3 that the influences of magnetic field as well as suction are to sufficiently raise the shear stress on the surface. High skin friction is noticed for the GQDs + Bi
2
Se
3
+Ag/Therminol VP-1 which is tri-hybrid case of nanofluid. While the skin frictions in pure and hybrid case is noticed to be low as equated to the ternary case. Suction in a ternary nanofluid flow involving graphene quantum dots (GQDs), bismuth selenide (Bi
2
Se
3 ), and silver (Ag) dispersed in Therminol VP-1 significantly effects the skin friction. Suction removes fluid from the near-wall region. It causes the thinning of the boundary Table 4 layer and suppressing flow separation. Variation in velocity with magnetic and suction parameter can be observed from Fig. 5 a–d
. Velocity is diminishing for both parameters in either case of nanofluid. The temperature
θ
( η )
is high when magnetic strength is increased but it is got decelerated when values of suction are increasing. The change is observed for all the proposed cases of nanofluids.

The presence of a magnetic field in the flow of the ternary nanofluid with thermal radiation can significantly enhance the temperature distribution. The magnetohydrodynamic damping caused by the magnetic field slows the flow and enhances thermal boundary layer thickness. This slowing of the fluid allows more time for heat transfer. Due to this reason the temperature of the fluid increases.

The thermal radiation is also one of the factors that take part in increasing the temperature, as shown in Fig. 6 a. It has been judged from the analysis of Fig. 6 b and c that both volume concentrations
Φ 2
of bismuth selenide (Bi
2
Se
3 ) and
Φ 3
of silver (Ag) tend to elevate the temperature in usual, hybridized and tri-hybridized case of nanofluid. It is examined here that the temperature is prominent in case of GQDs + Bi
2
Se
3
+Ag/Therminol VP-1 nanofluids. Whereas
θ
( η )
is comparatively smaller for GQDs/Therminol VP-1 (pure nanofluid) and GQDs + Bi
2
Se
3 /Therminol VP-1 (hybrid nanofluid). Bismuth selenide is a thermoelectric material. It contributes to heat transfer by promoting uniform temperature distribution due to its high anisotropic heat conduction. Silver is known for its exceptional thermal conductivity. It amplifies this effect by facilitating rapid heat diffusion within the fluid. When dispersed in Therminol VP-1, these nanoparticles synergize to boost heat transfer rates. Consequently, it results in an elevated temperature of the fluid. This effect is further intensified under the influence of thermal radiation.

The concentration
Φ 2
of bismuth selenide and
Φ 3
of silver both cause an increase int the heat transfer rate in all three cases of nanofluids (see Table 4). On the other hand, the influence of thermal radiation is to deteriorate the rate of heat transfer. GQDs enhance the thermal conductivity and stability of the base fluid. However, it improves the heat transfer rate as compared to pure Therminol VP-1. Adding Bi
2
Se
3 to GQDs further boosts the heat transfer rate due to its thermoelectric properties and high anisotropic thermal conductivity. The inclusion of silver in hybrid mixture possesses superior thermal conductivity. The combination of GQDs, Bi
2
Se
3 , and Ag provides maximum thermal performance by efficiently conducting and distributing heat throughout the fluid. This combination of ternary nanofluid is optimal for applications requiring high heat transfer rates.

The concentration profile
ϕ
( η )
downturns with chemical reaction, as depicted in Fig. 7 a. Contrarily, the activation energy
A E
substantially promotes the concentration in either case of nanofluid e.g., ternary, binary and pure nano fluid case (Fig. 7 b). The results of Table 5 evidently express the fact that a decrement in the mass transfer is due to the presence of activation energy in the flow. On the opposite, an enhancement in Sherwood number is noticed for the chemical reaction parameter. Activation energy impacts mass transfer by influencing the rate of molecular diffusion and reaction processes.

A higher activation energy increases the energy barrier for mass transfer processes like diffusion or chemical reactions. As a result, fewer molecules have the required energy to overcome this barrier. This phenomenon reduces the mass transfer rate. On the other side, species concentration enhances when activation energy
A E
is increased near the surface or reaction site. It happens because molecules accumulate due to the reduced rate of outward diffusion or reaction. The increasing values of
A E
lead to a higher local concentration but a diminished overall mass transfer rate. A quantitative analysis is presented in Table 6 for the heat transfer enhancement with an increase in the volume concentration
Φ 2
of bismuth selenide and
Φ 3
of silver.

The current research investigates the unique thermal characteristics of a ternary nanocomposite consisting of GQDs, Bi
2
Se
3 , and Ag particles suspended in Therminol VP-1. The inclusion of thermal radiation and activation energy provides valuable insights into temperature-sensitive processes. Our analysis examines three types of nano-compositions: mono, bi-hybrid, and trihybrid. To interpret thermal and flow behavior and assess the nonlinear effects of governing parameters, we integrate a recurrent neural network (RNN). Machine learning technique enable precise predictions of heat transfer performance and optimization of nanofluid-based thermal systems through data-driven approaches. This research seeks to enhance the understanding of how specific nanomaterial additives can improve heat transfer fluid performance and develop computational models for optimizing their behavior in energy systems. The potential results of the research are given below.
○
The influences of magnetic field as well as suction are to sufficiently raise the shear stress on the surface. High skin friction is noticed for the tri-hybrid (GQDs + Bi
2
Se
3
+Ag/Therminol VP-1) case of nanofluid.
○
The Recurrent neural network RNN are particularly suitable for modelling the trihybrid nanofluids due to the system's temporal and nonlinear nature.
○
A decrement in the mass transfer is due to the presence of activation energy in the flow. Contrarily, an enhancement in Sherwood number is noticed for the chemical reaction parameter.
○
Recurrent neural network incorporates training via backpropagation through time for gradient calculation which can face challenges like vanishing/exploding gradients.
○
The temperature
θ
( η )
is high when magnetic strength is increased but it is got decelerated when values of suction are increased.
○
The volume concentrations
Φ 2
of bismuth selenide (Bi
2
Se
3 ) and
Φ 3
of silver (Ag) tend to elevate the temperature in usual, hybridized and tri-hybridized case of nanofluid.
○
The activation energy
A E
substantially promotes the concentration in either case of nanofluid e.g., ternary, binary and pure nano fluid case.
○
The RNN predicts future states of temperature given initial conditions and driving forces.
Sohail Ahmad: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Hessa A. Alsalmah: Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported and funded by the Deanship of Scientific Research at Imam Mohammad Ibn Saud Islamic University (IMSIU) (grant number IMSIU-DDRSP2503).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Devi, S. P. A., Devi, S. S. U. Numerical investigation of hydromagnetic hybrid Cu-Al2O3/water nanofluid flow over a permeable stretching sheet with suction. Int. J. Nonlin. Sci. Numer. Simul., 17(5): 249-257, 2016.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Analysis of pure nanofluid (GO/Engine oil) and hybrid nanofluid (GO–Fe3O4/engine oil): novel thermal and magnetic features
Nanotechnol. Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ahmad, S., Ali, K., Ashraf, M., et al. Analysis of pure nanofluid (GO/engine oil) and hybrid nanofluid (GO-Fe3O4/engine oil): Novel thermal and magnetic features. Nanotechnol. Rev., 11: 2903-2915, 2022. https://doi.org/10.1515/ntrev-2022-0486.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Boundary-layer flow of a nanofluid past a stretching sheet
Int. J. Heat Mass Tran.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Khan, W. A., Pop, I. Boundary-layer flow of a nanofluid past a stretching sheet. Int. J. Heat Mass Transf., 53: 2477-2483, 2010.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: CFD analysis of paraffin-based hybrid (Co–Au) and trihybrid (Co–Au–ZrO2) nanofluid flow through a porous medium
Nanotechnol. Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yang, D., Ahmad, S., Ali, K., Algarni, S., Alqahtani, T., Jamshed, W., Hussain, S. M., Irshad, K., Ahmad, H. CFD analysis of paraffin-based hybrid (Co-Au) and trihybrid (Co-Au-ZrO2) nanofluid flow through a porous medium. Nanotechnol. Rev., 13(1): 20240024, 2024. https://doi.org/10.1515/ntrev-2024-0024.
